name: Serge Chaloff
abstract: Serge Chaloff (November 24, 1923 – July 16, 1957) was an American jazz baritone saxophonist. One of bebop's earliest baritone saxophonists, Chaloff has been described as 'the most expressive and openly emotive baritone saxophonist jazz has ever witnessed' with a tone varying 'between a light but almost inaudible whisper to a great sonoro…

concerts.label: Woody Herman and His Orchestra and The Nat King Cole Trio
Woody Herman and His Orchestra and The Nat King Cole Trio

about_quotes.speaker: Ellis Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: Well, you know, wasn’t no sense in talking about Serge Chaloff, because I’d struck out, if you don’t know them.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ellis Marsalis — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: Serge Chaloff and Rouse and Buddy DeFranco and I were the front line in Basie’s band
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Woody Herman
about_quotes.evidence: I did hire Serge Chaloff, because I heard about him, and I figured he'd be a good -- he'd be the right baritone for that period.

about_quotes.speaker: Trummy Young
about_quotes.evidence: Bothwell and I were actually with Boyd Raeburn together. We were in Boyd Raeburn's band together. So was Serge Chaloff

about_quotes.speaker: George Wein
about_quotes.evidence: There were other musicians a year ahead of me, some great musicians: Serge Chaloff and Hal McKusick... They left school as soon as they could and went on the road with Tommy Reynolds and bands like that.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Wein — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: Buddy DeFranco and Serge Chaloff. Well, Gus Johnson had been in the band before... he did get a lot of static at places that he played. Not that the guys that he had playing with him weren’t capable
about_quotes.doc_title: Harry Edison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
